questions.stem: Find the error: She not only (A) / lost her wallet (B) / but also (C) / her mobile phone. (D)
options: A - should be 'She lost not only'
questions.solution: A - should be 'She lost not only'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The evidence ____ against the accused.

questions.stem: Find the error: The data (A) / collected from (B) / the site is (C) / being analysed. (D)

questions.stem: The document ____ the terms of the agreement.

questions.stem: The manager asked ____ the report was ready.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: solve / team / the / to / problem / together / worked
options: The team worked together to solve the problem
Worked together the team to solve the problem
Together the team worked to solve the problem
The team worked together to solve the problem
To solve the problem the team together worked
questions.solution: The team worked together to solve the problem

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Searching a skewed (degenerate) binary search tree of n nodes takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: A fully skewed BST behaves like a linked list, giving O(n) search.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">XOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 12-bit register can represent how many distinct values?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: IP addresses at the network layer
MAC addresses at the data-link layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NAND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case lookup time of a hash table (all keys colliding) is:
questions.solution: If every key hashes to the same bucket, lookup degrades to scanning a list — O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 11, push 14, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which sorting algorithm is in-place (O(1) extra space)?
questions.solution: Quicksort sorts within the array; standard merge sort needs O(n) auxiliary space.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock requires NO preemption; allowing preemption actually prevents deadlock.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of sorting algorithms is correct?
options: Quicksort is stable; merge sort is not
Merge sort is stable; standard quicksort is not
questions.solution: Merge sort preserves equal-key order (stable); typical quicksort does not.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /24 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 20 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about processes and threads is INCORRECT?
options: Threads of the same process have separate heaps
Each thread has its own stack
Context switching between threads is cheaper than between processes
Threads share the process's code and data
questions.solution: Threads share the heap; only stacks and registers are per-thread.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 500 MB at 100 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for on-disk databases because they:
options: Are shallow (high fan-out), reducing disk accesses
questions.solution: High branching factor keeps B-trees shallow, minimizing expensive disk seeks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bubble sort has a worst-case time complexity of:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes ~n²/2 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 40), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 10, 15, 23, 40, 65.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">40</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">15</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">65</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">10</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">23</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a direct-mapped cache, each main-memory block can be placed in:
questions.solution: Direct mapping fixes each block to one line (block mod #lines), unlike associative caches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(5) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 8, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about TCP and UDP is INCORRECT?
options: UDP has lower overhead than TCP
questions.solution: UDP is best-effort with no ordering or reliability guarantees.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11110000 equals:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 4 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
At program load time, all at once
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 16 bits occupies how many bytes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about keys is INCORRECT?
options: A table can have multiple candidate keys
A foreign key can be null
A primary key may contain null values
questions.solution: A primary key must be non-null and unique.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 1011₂ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 6-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quicksort attains its O(n²) worst case when:
options: The pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
The pivot is always the median
The array is large but random
questions.solution: Maximally unbalanced partitions (e.g. sorted input with first/last pivot) give O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 8 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 8KB = 2^13 bytes, so the offset field is 13 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 4-bit register can represent how many distinct values?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 1 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 1KB = 2^10 bytes, so the offset field is 10 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Given the network 192.168.1.0/26, the number of usable host addresses per subnet is:
questions.solution: /26 leaves 6 host bits ⇒ 2⁶ − 2 = 62 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 200 ns, page-fault rate = 0.001, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">OR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: With consistently poor pivots (e.g. already-sorted input) quicksort degrades to O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 2 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TCP connection is established using a:
questions.solution: TCP setup uses SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK; teardown uses a four-way exchange.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 20 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Executes line by line at run time
Translates the whole program before execution
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as AND.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 170 150" width="170" height="150"><text x="26" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">A</text><text x="66" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">B</text><text x="112" y="35" font-size="11" font-weight="bold">Y</text><line x1="20" y1="40" x2="150" y2="40" stroke="#475569"/><text x="30" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="55" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="55" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="75" font-size="11">0</text><text x="70" y="75" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="75" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="95" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="95" font-size="11">0</text><text x="120" y="95" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">0</text><text x="30" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="70" y="115" font-size="11">1</text><text x="120" y="115" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">1</text></svg>
figure.caption: Truth table

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
Speeds up both reads and writes equally
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 11, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles on resize has an amortized insertion cost of:
questions.solution: Doubling makes total work O(n) over n inserts, i.e. amortized O(1) each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear search of an unsorted array of n items takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: It may examine every element, so worst-case time is O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For every nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) there exists:
questions.solution: NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same (regular) languages — the subset construction proves it.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /28 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a 5-stage instruction pipeline, a data hazard arises when:
options: An instruction needs a result not yet produced by an earlier instruction
questions.solution: Data hazards occur from dependencies; they are mitigated by forwarding or stalls.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 8, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Has a completely separate address space
Shares the address space of its process
Has its own copy of global data
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 256 elements needs at most how many comparisons?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 100 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 2K memory locations?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 140" width="220" height="140"><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="160" y2="30" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="30" x2="40" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="160" y1="30" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="40" y1="110" x2="160" y2="110" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">A</text><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">B</text><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="40" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">C</text><circle cx="160" cy="110" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="160" y="114" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">D</text></svg>
figure.caption: Undirected graph (4 nodes)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: Solvable in polynomial time for sure
In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 50), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 20, 25, 33, 50, 70.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="80" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="130" y1="30" x2="190" y2="75" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="50" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="80" y1="75" x2="110" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="30" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="130" y="34" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">50</text><circle cx="80" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="80" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><circle cx="190" cy="75" r="16" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="190" y="79" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">70</text><circle cx="50" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="50" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">20</text><circle cx="110" cy="120" r="16" fill="#dcfce7" stroke="#16a34a"/><text x="110" y="124" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">33</text></svg>
figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths correctly only when edge weights are:
questions.solution: Dijkstra assumes non-negative weights; negative edges require Bellman–Ford.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 1F₁₆ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For large n, which growth ordering is correct?
options: log n grows faster than n
questions.solution: Exponential 2ⁿ eventually dominates any polynomial like n².

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 8 elements is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average and worst-case time complexity of merge sort is:
questions.solution: Merge sort always divides and merges in O(n log n), and it is stable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 100×20 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 200 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 8 ms. The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of n elements runs in:
questions.solution: Each comparison halves the search space, giving logarithmic time — but the array must be sorted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The base (radix) of the hexadecimal number system is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 100 slots holds 45 keys. Its load factor is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.95 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:
